result: I keep thinking about the way afternoons used to smell—sun-warm bricks, the ghost of breakfast bacon dug into the curtains, the aluminum tang of sprinkler water baking off the lawn. These molecules occupy some quantum shelf inside my skull; I can summon them like cats and they come, even when today’s weather is featureless, hushed in the gray of a laptop screen. Memory, turns out to be an atmospheric art: light, moisture, ions of emotion all conspiring to recreate a room you walked out of decades ago. And yet it remains impossible to reenter; you can tour the hallways only as a vapor yourself, pressed against the wallpaper like an exhaled breath. We talk about nostalgia as if it were sentimental weakness—something that keeps us from leveling up into better, more evolved creatures. I think it’s braver to admit being haunted. After all, every instant is evaporating behind us; the air is full of crisp, colored negatives of our days floating up like confetti, organized by no librarian. Most will scatter, some will dissolve in rain, possibly one fleck will lodge in a stranger’s lung and inspire inexplicable tenderness at 3 a.m. The only responsible choice is to keep inhaling. Breathing is an act of curation and surrender at once: letting yesterday circulate through us again—part perfume part nerve gas—and releasing it transformed. The lungs are small museums with revolving doors. Step outside now; see how the light leans gold on the brick, how tomorrow is already friable in your palm.
